caveats: a: The BLS table covers all NAICS 335100 lighting manufacturing, not only commercial, industrial, and institutional fixtures.
a: Occupation shares are not task shares.
a: The balance of engineering, specification support, and direct production differs across standard and custom manufacturers.
rho: Direct task-level AI adoption data were unavailable for the six-digit industry.
rho: Custom engineered and controls-rich products may see high augmentation but slower autonomous substitution because errors affect projects and compliance.

e: Value capture varies with custom content, brand and specification strength, channel concentration, controls software ownership, and the ability to translate faster work into lower cost or additional project wins.
s5: DOE adoption figures are older than the run date.
s5: Integrated controls may expand fixture value for capable manufacturers while displacing undifferentiated products.
s5: Service-life extension lowers replacement frequency but does not remove renovation or performance-upgrade demand.
q: No direct retention data were available.
q: Quality is strongest in proprietary, custom, or tightly specified applications and weaker in commodity troffers and linear fixtures.
d5: Construction spending includes many categories unrelated to lighting and reflects price as well as volume.
d5: Efficiency and control retrofits depend on energy prices, incentives, capital budgets, and code adoption.
d5: Long LED lives can defer ordinary replacement after retrofit waves mature.
o: Highly standardized manufacturers may centralize more support work than custom architectural or controls-rich firms.
o: Small firms may already operate with combined roles and thin management layers.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, specification, distributor, OEM, retrofit, and replacement revenue from electrical distributors, contractors, architects and engineers, facility owners, industrial operators, institutions, energy-service companies, and public agencies. Repeat demand reflects specifications, product families, renovation cycles, warranties, controls compatibility, and installed-base replacement.
lens.excluded_activities: Residential, roadway, vehicular, traffic-signal, and nonelectric lighting; LED semiconductor and standalone bulb manufacturing; current-carrying wiring devices; controls-only software or hardware vendors; distributors, lighting designers, contractors, and installers without principal fixture manufacturing; and captive divisions not independently marketable.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers of electric luminaires and fixtures principally designed for commercial, industrial, and institutional buildings, including troffers, linear ambient fixtures, high- and low-bay luminaires, architectural fixtures, task and specialty building fixtures, and integrated LED and control-ready products.

a.bridge: Commercial fixture makers share the engineering, production, commercial, and administrative functions measured in the 335100 occupation table. This segment likely has somewhat more technical specification, photometric, and controls-integration work than residential lighting, but the ancestor data remain the closest occupation proxy and are applied with a broad range.
caveats: The BLS table covers all NAICS 335100 lighting manufacturing, not only commercial, industrial, and institutional fixtures.
Occupation shares are not task shares.
The balance of engineering, specification support, and direct production differs across standard and custom manufacturers.
a.change_evidence: Engineering copilots, document extraction, automated submittal preparation, photometric analysis support, and sales and service assistants broaden addressable tasks in specification-heavy workflows.
a.rationale: BLS data for broader electric lighting equipment manufacturing show production occupations at 42.42% of employment, alongside meaningful management, business, computer, engineering, sales, design, and office-support groups. Commercial fixture manufacturers expose specification work, photometric and application support, engineering, controls integration, quoting, submittals, sourcing, planning, customer service, and administration to AI; fabrication, assembly, testing, finishing, packaging, and materials handling remain physical.

caveats: Construction spending includes many categories unrelated to lighting and reflects price as well as volume.
Efficiency and control retrofits depend on energy prices, incentives, capital budgets, and code adoption.
Long LED lives can defer ordinary replacement after retrofit waves mature.
d5.change_evidence: Census reported May 2026 private nonresidential construction at a $738.7 billion annual rate, down 0.3% from April, while public construction rose and educational construction reached $113.4 billion. DOE identifies large remaining savings potential in low/high-bay and linear commercial fixtures and connected controls.
d5.rationale: Retrofits, tightening efficiency expectations, connected controls, public and institutional construction, industrial facilities, and replacement of legacy fluorescent systems support demand. Nonresidential construction cyclicality, long LED service lives, office-space uncertainty, and import competition restrain growth.

caveats: Value capture varies with custom content, brand and specification strength, channel concentration, controls software ownership, and the ability to translate faster work into lower cost or additional project wins.
e.change_evidence: Structured product, specification, and test-report data make workflow tools deployable, while qualification and warranty obligations keep valuable domain expertise inside the firm.
e.rationale: Manufacturers can retain gains through faster bids and submittals, reduced application-engineering and support time, improved sourcing and planning, fewer documentation errors, and higher throughput. Capture is moderated by distributor and project-buyer bargaining power, competitive tenders, certification and software costs, and the need to preserve technical sales and field support.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3351: labor compensation (L02) $3,249.0M / sectoral output (T30) $10,230.1M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $422,130 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 3351); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Highly standardized manufacturers may centralize more support work than custom architectural or controls-rich firms.
Small firms may already operate with combined roles and thin management layers.
o.change_evidence: Increasingly technical performance and controls requirements preserve specialist review and product accountability even as routine documentation and analytical work become more automated.
o.rationale: AI can streamline specification support, engineering documentation, quotations, project administration, and customer service, but firms retain accountable leaders for product safety, photometrics, controls, manufacturing, quality, warranties, distributor relationships, and project delivery. Organizational displacement should be limited.

caveats: No direct retention data were available.
Quality is strongest in proprietary, custom, or tightly specified applications and weaker in commodity troffers and linear fixtures.
q.change_evidence: Rising efficacy, controls, warranty, and documented-performance requirements favor qualified incumbents, while searchable digital catalogs and standardized technical criteria make alternatives easier to compare.
q.rationale: Specifications, approved product lists, photometric files, code compliance, controls compatibility, warranties, distributor stocking, matching product families, and the risk of project delays support persistence. Competition remains substantial: DOE cites a qualified-products database with more than 120,000 solid-state lighting products, and project buyers can re-bid standardized fixtures.

caveats: Direct task-level AI adoption data were unavailable for the six-digit industry.
Custom engineered and controls-rich products may see high augmentation but slower autonomous substitution because errors affect projects and compliance.
rho.change_evidence: Commercial luminaires increasingly combine efficiency, controls, and documented performance attributes, expanding digital workflows that AI can assist while also increasing the need for verified engineering outputs.
rho.rationale: AI can augment fixture selection, photometric and application analysis, CAD and engineering support, bill-of-material review, code and specification research, quotation, submittals, project tracking, technical support, and administration. Human accountability remains important for optical and thermal performance, electrical safety, controls interoperability, testing, manufacturing, quality, and project commitments.

caveats: DOE adoption figures are older than the run date.
Integrated controls may expand fixture value for capable manufacturers while displacing undifferentiated products.
Service-life extension lowers replacement frequency but does not remove renovation or performance-upgrade demand.
s5.change_evidence: DOE reported connected controls on only 0.2% of installed lighting in 2018 but estimated substantial potential energy savings from wider deployment. FEMP guidance notes LED luminaires' compatibility with occupancy sensors, task tuning, and dimming and their very long service lives.
s5.rationale: Buildings still require physical luminaires, but value can migrate from standalone fixtures toward integrated controls, sensors, building-management platforms, modular systems, and direct-import products. Long-lived LEDs reduce maintenance replacement, while retrofit and controls upgrades create offsetting demand for new, connected luminaires.

principal_driver: Efficiency retrofits and integration of lighting with sensors and connected building controls.
principal_weakness: Long product lives and intense specification-based competition in standardized fixture categories.
